title: Feelings & Emotions
description: Express how you feel naturally — with the right feminine verb endings.
culturalNote: Hindi verbs change based on the speaker's gender. As a woman, you'll use -gayi (not -gaya), -rahi hoon (not -raha hoon), -hoon (same for both). This lesson drills the feminine forms so they become automatic for you.

exercises.english: I got tired / I'm worn out (feminine)
exercises.note: Feminine: 'thak gayi'. Masculine: 'thak gaya'. 'Bahut thak gayi yaar' = I'm so tired, man. 'Thak gayi hoon' = I am tired (ongoing state). Very commonly used.
'Bura laga' = it felt bad (about a situation). 'Mujhe bura laga' = I felt bad. 'Usse bura lagega' = it will make her feel bad. Gender-neutral — 'laga' doesn't change for speaker's gender.

exercises.english: It felt good / I liked it
exercises.note: The positive version. 'Bahut acha laga' = it felt really good. 'Mujhe acha laga ki tum aayi' = I'm glad you came. Versatile — use after any positive experience.

exercises.prompt: After a great dinner with friends, you want to say 'I really enjoyed it / it felt great'. What do you say?

exercises.english: I'm getting angry / I'm getting irritated
exercises.spanish: Me está dando rabia / Me estoy enojando
exercises.note: Literally 'anger is coming'. 'Gussa' = anger. 'Mujhe gussa aa raha hai' = anger is coming to me = I'm getting angry. 'Gussa mat ho' = don't get angry. 'Bahut gussa aaya' = I got very angry.
Literally 'fear is feeling'. 'Dar' = fear. 'Mujhe dar lag raha hai' = I'm feeling scared. 'Darr mat' = don't be afraid. 'Koi dar nahi' = nothing to be afraid of.

exercises.prompt: You're nervous before a big presentation. How do you express 'I'm scared'?

exercises.english: It was fun / I had a blast
exercises.note: 'Maza' = fun/pleasure. 'Bahut maza aaya' = it was so much fun. 'Maza aa raha hai' = I'm having fun right now. 'Maza nahi aaya' = it wasn't fun. Essential phrase for after any outing.
Feminine: 'bore ho gayi'. Masculine: 'bore ho gaya'. 'Bore ho rahi hoon' = I'm getting bored (ongoing). Code-switch with English 'bore' + Hindi grammar — perfectly natural in Delhi.

exercises.sentence: Yaar woh movie itni slow thi, main _____ completely.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: Man that movie was so slow, I _____ completely.

exercises.note: Feminine continuous: 'ro rahi hoon'. Masculine: 'ro raha hoon'. 'Kyu ro rahi ho?' = why are you crying? 'Rona mat' = don't cry. 'Rone ka mann hai' = I feel like crying.
'Khush' = happy. 'Hoon' = I am. 'Bahut khush hoon' = I'm very happy. 'Khush raho' = stay happy (blessing/farewell). 'Tujhe khush dekhke khushi hoti hai' = seeing you happy makes me happy.

exercises.english: I'm worried / I'm troubled / I'm stressed
exercises.note: 'Pareshaan' = worried/troubled. 'Mat ho pareshaan' = don't worry. 'Woh mujhe pareshaan karta hai' = he troubles/bothers me. 'Bahut pareshaan hoon' = I'm really stressed out.

exercises.prompt: You're really worried about an exam tomorrow. How do you express this?

exercises.english: I don't feel like it / My heart isn't in it
exercises.spanish: No tengo ganas / No me apetece
exercises.note: 'Mann' = heart. Literally 'heart isn't doing'. This is how you say 'I don't feel like it' in Hindi. 'Mann nahi kar raha kuch karne ka' = I don't feel like doing anything. Extremely common.

exercises.sentence: Aaj khaana banana _____. Pizza order karte hain.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: I don't feel like cooking today. Let's order pizza.

exercises.instruction: Match the feeling to its Hindi phrase

exercises.english: I'm not feeling well / I don't feel good
exercises.note: Code-switched with English 'feel' + Hindi grammar. Completely natural in Delhi. Can mean physically unwell or emotionally not okay. 'Kya hua? Theek nahi feel ho raha?' = what happened, are you not feeling okay?

exercises.prompt: After a long hard week, you text your friend: 'I'm so tired and worn out.' What's the most natural Hindi phrase?
